metadata.title: Why Only 0.7% of a Cancer Nanoparticle Reaches the Tumor
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Nanomedicine candidate 02. Source chapter: cancer-nanomedicine/chapters/01-what-counts-as-cancer-nanomedicine.md (the dose-loss chain / delivery funnel mechanism only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; funnel drain moves show dose loss step by step
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, teal = dose reaching tumor / surviving dose, crimson = each loss step / lost dose, gold highlighter on the 0.7% number

metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = dose reaching tumor; CRIMSON = lost dose / each loss step; GOLD = the 0.7% number
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: Cut DLS/TEM, polydispersity, two-liposomes case, EPR mechanism detail, the 0.7% metric debate. Keep funnel and the number only.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Wonder register (voices/wonder/VOICE.md + MEDHAVY.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the MEDHAVY outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Medhavy.com
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A researcher injects tumor-targeted nanoparticles into a mouse. Only 0.7 percent reaches the tumor. The other 99.3 percent goes somewhere else — and that somewhere else is the whole story.

card.copy: Why Only 0.7% of a Cancer Nanoparticle Reaches the Tumor

beats.narration_text: The particle was engineered carefully. It carries a targeting ligand that binds receptors overexpressed on cancer cells. In cell culture, it kills at a fraction of the normal dose. So why does almost none of it arrive?

props.headline: The particle was designed carefully. It carries a targeting ligand that…

beats.scene_description: a vial of nanoparticle solution on a laboratory bench, beside a syringe — editorial newsprint collage style, flat desaturated plate, slow push-in on the vial label
beats.image_prompt: B02, glass vial of clear nanoparticle solution and a syringe on a white laboratory bench, one vial and one syringe, horizontal composition, flat overhead light, no shadows, desaturated documentary photograph pinned to aged newsprint like an editorial clipping, no people, no text visible on label
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL-ai] the engineered particle — vial and syringe on bench

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL-ai] the engineered particle — vial and syringe on bench

beats.narration_text: Here's what we're figuring out. A tumor-targeted nanoparticle is injected into the bloodstream. The targeting ligand is working. And still, only 0.7 percent reaches the tumor. What is the body doing to the other 99.3?

card.copy: A tumor-targeted nanoparticle is injected. Only 0.7% reaches the tumor. The targeting ligand is working. So why does so little arrive?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] gap formula question on screen

beats.narration_text: Here's why: between the syringe and the tumor cell, the dose is lost at every step. Not one step — five sequential steps. Fail at any of them and the particle delivers nothing. Each step is a separate barrier the particle has to clear.

production_viz.label: five steps from syringe to tumor
production_viz.mechanic: a wide TEAL bar labeled 'Injected Dose' sits left; five crimson step labels appear sequentially to the right, with arrows between them, establishing the chain before the drain begins
production_viz.note: establish the chain structure — no numbers yet

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] five-step chain from injection to tumor cell

beats.narration_text: Step one: survive circulation. The liver and spleen are clearing foreign particles from the blood within minutes of injection. Most of the dose never gets a second chance — it's gone before it can reach the tumor.

production_viz.label: step 1 — circulation loss
production_viz.mechanic: the full TEAL dose bar shrinks from the right as a CRIMSON loss chip 'CLEARED BY LIVER / SPLEEN' appears; a TEAL remainder label updates; the 'drain' move — bar clips leftward
production_viz.note: first drain beat; wide bar shrinks to show loss at step 1

beats.narration_text: Step two: escape the vessel. Particles must exit the bloodstream into tumor tissue through leaky vessel walls. Many degrade or get physically trapped before they can make that crossing.

production_viz.label: step 2 — extravasation loss
production_viz.mechanic: the surviving TEAL bar from step 1 drains further; CRIMSON chip 'FAILS TO EXTRAVASATE' appears; TEAL remainder bar narrows again
production_viz.note: second drain beat; continuing the funnel

beats.narration_text: Steps three, four, and five: penetrate the dense tumor matrix, get taken up by cells, and release the payload inside. Each step cuts the arriving dose again — and we end up at that 0.7 percent.

production_viz.label: steps 3-4-5 — penetration, uptake, release
production_viz.mechanic: three more sequential drain moves compress into one beat; three crimson loss chips appear in sequence as the TEAL bar narrows to a thin sliver; GOLD label '0.7%' appears on the sliver at the end
production_viz.note: the funnel resolves to 0.7%; gold highlights the final number

beats.narration_text: Here's the counterintuitive part. Adding a targeting ligand only helps at step four — cell uptake. A particle cleared from circulation in the first hour never reaches step four. The ligand never gets a chance to bind.

production_viz.label: targeting only fixes step 4
production_viz.mechanic: the five-step chain from B04 reappears; a GOLD bracket appears under step 4 labeled 'targeting ligand helps here'; steps 1-3 are ringed or marked CRIMSON as 'still lost'; the message is visual — fix late step but early steps still kill
production_viz.note: key implication beat — targeting does not fix early loss

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] targeting only addresses step 4 — early steps dominate loss

beats.narration_text: The particle was not misbehaving. The targeting ligand was binding correctly. The delivery chain failed upstream — long before the ligand had any role. You can't fix a step-one problem with a step-four solution.

card.copy: Most of what is injected does not arrive. Adding a targeting ligand to a particle that fails at step one does not solve the problem.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] quote — targeting cannot fix an early-step failure

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] quote — targeting cannot fix an early-step failure

beats.narration_text: Take 100 illustrative units — let's walk through where they go. Eighteen are cleared by spleen and liver in hour one. Fourteen more degrade in circulation. Twelve exit the vessel but cannot penetrate the tumor matrix. Fifty-five reach non-tumor tissue. One unit — 0.7 — reaches the tumor and releases payload.

production_viz.label: 100 units tracked — illustrative
production_viz.mechanic: two-column layout: left column CRIMSON header 'lost at each step' with a breakdown (18 + 14 + 12 + 55 = 99.3); right column TEAL header 'reaches tumor' showing 0.7; MONO font for numbers; a small MONO note bar at bottom reads 'numbers illustrative'
production_viz.note: STANDARD EXAMPLE template — illustrative numbers labeled clearly

beats.narration_text: A perfect targeting ligand solves one step of five. The funnel leaks everywhere upstream. Until those upstream steps are measured and fixed, 0.7 percent is the answer — not a flaw in the ligand, but a problem earlier in the chain.

card.copy: Why only 0.7%? Five steps lose dose before the ligand has a chance.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — question answered, topic named
